vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's enforce_for_root parameter controls requirements for enforcing password complexity for the root user. Enable the enforce_for_root setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the root user to use complex passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then if [ -e "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*enforce_for_root/Id" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" else touch "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" cp "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "enforce_for_root" >> "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: The finding is related to password complexity for the root user, which falls under the safe_to_remediate category according to the policy.

execution_details.stdout:
# Configuration for systemwide password quality limits
# Defaults:
#
# Number of characters in the new password that must not be present in the
# old password.
# difok = 1
#
# Minimum acceptable size for the new password (plus one if
# credits are not disabled which is the default). (See pam_cracklib manual.)
# Cannot be set to lower value than 6.
# minlen = 8
#
# The maximum credit for having digits in the new password. If less than 0
# it is the minimum number of digits in the new password.
#
# The maximum credit for having uppercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of uppercase characters in the new
# password.
# ucredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having lowercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of lowercase characters in the new
# password.
# lcredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having other characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of other characters in the new
# password.
# ocredit = 0
#
# The minimum number of required classes of characters for the new
# password (digits, uppercase, lowercase, others).
# minclass = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive same characters in the new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxrepeat = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive characters of the same class in the
# new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxclassrepeat = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the passwd entry GECOS string of the user.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# gecoscheck = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the cracklib dictionary.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# dictcheck = 1
#
# Whether to check if it contains the user name in some form.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# usercheck = 1
#
# Length of substrings from the username to check for in the password
# The check is enabled if the value is greater than 0 and usercheck is enabled.
# usersubstr = 0
#
# Whether the check is enforced by the PAM module and possibly other
# applications.
# The new password is rejected if it fails the check and the value is not 0.
# enforcing = 1
#
# Path to the cracklib dictionaries. Default is to use the cracklib default.
# dictpath =
#
# Prompt user at most N times before returning with error. The default is 1.
# retry = 3
#
# Enforces pwquality checks on the root user password.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# enforce_for_root
#
# Skip testing the password quality for users that are not present in the
# /etc/passwd file.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# local_users_only
# Per : Set dictcheck = 1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
dictcheck = 1
# Per : Set difok = 8 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
difok = 8
# enforce_for_root
# Per : Set lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
lcredit = 0
# Per : Set maxclassrepeat = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
maxclassrepeat = 1
# Per : Set maxrepeat = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
maxrepeat = 0
# Per : Set minclass = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
minclass = 4
# Per : Set minlen = 15 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
minlen = 6
# Per : Set ocredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ocredit = 0
# Per : Set retry = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
retry = 3
# Per : Set ucredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ucredit = 0

dcredit = 1

previous_attempts.scan_output: Vulnerability openscap_016 not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
The finding "accounts_password_pam_enforce_root" is related to password complexity for the root user. The pam_pwquality module's enforce_for_root parameter controls requirements for enforcing password complexity for the root user. To fix this finding, you need to enable the enforce_for_root setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.

Here are the steps to fix this finding:

1. Read the /etc/security/pwquality.conf file to check if it already contains the enforce_for_root parameter.
2. If the parameter is already present, sed -i "/^enforce_for_root/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf will delete the existing line to avoid duplication.
3. Then, printf '%s\n' 'enforce_for_root' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf will append the enforce_for_root parameter to the end of the file.
4. Finally, run grep -q '^enforce_for_root' /etc/security/pwquality.conf to verify that the parameter has been added correctly.
5. Call the scan function to confirm that the fix has been successful.

After these steps, the finding "accounts_password_pam_enforce_root" should be resolved, and the scan should return a "pass" result.

enforce_for_root

execution_details.stdout:
# Configuration for systemwide password quality limits
# Defaults:
#
# Number of characters in the new password that must not be present in the
# old password.
# difok = 1
#
# Minimum acceptable size for the new password (plus one if
# credits are not disabled which is the default). (See pam_cracklib manual.)
# Cannot be set to lower value than 6.
# minlen = 8
#
# The maximum credit for having digits in the new password. If less than 0
# it is the minimum number of digits in the new password.
#
# The maximum credit for having uppercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of uppercase characters in the new
# password.
# ucredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having lowercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of lowercase characters in the new
# password.
# lcredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having other characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of other characters in the new
# password.
# ocredit = 0
#
# The minimum number of required classes of characters for the new
# password (digits, uppercase, lowercase, others).
# minclass = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive same characters in the new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxrepeat = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive characters of the same class in the
# new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxclassrepeat = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the passwd entry GECOS string of the user.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# gecoscheck = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the cracklib dictionary.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# dictcheck = 1
#
# Whether to check if it contains the user name in some form.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# usercheck = 1
#
# Length of substrings from the username to check for in the password
# The check is enabled if the value is greater than 0 and usercheck is enabled.
# usersubstr = 0
#
# Whether the check is enforced by the PAM module and possibly other
# applications.
# The new password is rejected if it fails the check and the value is not 0.
# enforcing = 1
#
# Path to the cracklib dictionaries. Default is to use the cracklib default.
# dictpath =
#
# Prompt user at most N times before returning with error. The default is 1.
# retry = 3
#
# Enforces pwquality checks on the root user password.
# Enabled if the option is present.
enforce_for_root = 1
#
# Skip testing the password quality for users that are not present in the
# /etc/passwd file.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# local_users_only
# Per : Set dictcheck = 1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
dictcheck = 1
# Per : Set difok = 8 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
difok = 8
enforce_for_root = 1
# Per : Set lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
lcredit = 0
# Per : Set maxclassrepeat = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
maxclassrepeat = 1
# Per : Set maxrepeat = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
maxrepeat = 0
# Per : Set minclass = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
minclass = 4
# Per : Set minlen = 15 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
minlen = 6
# Per : Set ocredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ocredit = 0
# Per : Set retry = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
retry = 3
# Per : Set ucredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ucredit = 0

dcredit = 1
enforce_for_root = 1

llm_verdict.message:
The output of the script indicates that the vulnerability openscap_016 still exists after the remediation attempt, and the scan result is "fail". This suggests that the remediation was not successful in completely resolving the vulnerability. 

To further troubleshoot, it would be helpful to examine the /etc/security/pwquality.conf file after the sed command to ensure that the enforce_for_root parameter was properly updated. Additionally, checking the system logs for any error messages related to the pam_pwquality module may provide more insight into why the vulnerability still exists. 

It's also important to note that the sed command used in the remediation script assumes that the enforce_for_root parameter already exists in the /etc/security/pwquality.conf file. If this parameter does not exist, the sed command will not add it to the file. In this case, a different approach, such as appending the parameter to the end of the file, may be necessary. 

Further review and testing of the remediation script would be necessary to determine the root cause of the issue and to develop a corrected remediation approach.

review_feedback: Remediation did not successfully set enforce_for_root=1; missing value assignment and does not preserve file formatting or permissions. Suggestions: Add full directive with appropriate value, e.g., enforce_for_root = 1; Use a robust config editing method that preserves comments and formatting; Maintain original file permissions and ownership when modifying /etc/security/pwquality.conf; Include explicit validation that the setting is applied and that PAM reloads successfully

concerns: The added line only contains the key "enforce_for_root" without an assigned value (e.g., enforce_for_root=1), which may not actually enforce password complexity for root.
The script overwrites a backup file without preserving original file permissions and ownership.
The remediation uses a simplistic sed/printf approach that may not handle existing comments or alternative syntaxes robustly.
suggested_improvements: Set the full directive with an appropriate value, e.g., "enforce_for_root = 1", to ensure the requirement is actually applied.
Use a more idempotent and robust method for editing the configuration, such as a dedicated config parser or careful quoting to preserve existing formatting.
Preserve original file permissions, ownership, and any comments when modifying /etc/security/pwquality.conf.
Add explicit checks to ensure the value is set correctly and report any failures during remediation.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: Remediation did not successfully set enforce_for_root=1; missing value assignment and does not preserve file formatting or permissions.
concerns: Does not preserve original file permissions, ownership, or comments
Uses fragile sed patterns that may not handle all existing syntaxes
No verification that PAM enforces the setting after change
suggested_improvements: Add full directive with appropriate value, e.g., enforce_for_root = 1
Use a robust config editing method that preserves comments and formatting
Maintain original file permissions and ownership when modifying /etc/security/pwquality.conf
Include explicit validation that the setting is applied and that PAM reloads successfully
